name: Walla Walla
image_alt: Large round pale yellow sweet onion
description: The most celebrated sweet onion in American cuisine. Produces very large, mild, flat-to-round bulbs with pale yellow skin and white flesh. So low in pungency it can be eaten like an apple.
history: Brought to Washington's Walla Walla Valley by a French soldier named Peter Pieri from Corsica in the 1900s. Local farmers selected for sweetness over generations. The Walla Walla Sweet became federally recognized as a PGI product in 1995.

good.reason: Mutual pest deterrence — classic companion pairing
Efficient use of space, different root zones

seasonal_quirks: A long-day onion — requires 14–16 hours of daylight to bulb. Plant in fall in mild climates for spring harvest, or start indoors very early for summer harvest. High water content means shorter storage than pungent types — use within 2–3 months.
harvest_cues: Tops will fall over naturally when bulbs are mature. Cure in a warm, dry place for 2–3 weeks before storage. Skin should be papery and dry.
variety_notes: The sweetness comes from low pyruvate content — the compound responsible for onion pungency and eye irritation. High water content makes fresh use far superior to cooking.
preservation: Short storage life — 2–3 months maximum. Does not pickle as well as pungent types.
